FirstP.subgroup_info:
The point of view refers to the perspective used to write the feedback message: 
 - 1st person: Written from the instructor's perspective using 'I' statements. 
 - 2nd person: Addresses the student directly using 'you'.
 - 3rd person: Focused on the work itself rather than the instructor or student.
FirstP.name: First Person
FirstP.short: Feedback is written from the instructor's perspective, using 'I' statements.
FirstP.good_examples:
1) I think you only differentiated part of the product here. It looks like the product rule might be missing.
2) When I look at this, it seems like the derivative is only accounting for part of the product. Did you apply the full product rule?
FirstP.bad_examples:
1) I notice that the derivative appears to include only part of the product.
2) I observe that the product rule is not applied.

SecondP.subgroup_info:
The point of view refers to the perspective used to write the feedback message: 
 - 1st person: Written from the instructor's perspective using 'I' statements. 
 - 2nd person: Addresses the student directly using 'you'.
 - 3rd person: Focused on the work itself rather than the instructor or student.
SecondP.name: Second Person
SecondP.short: Feedback addresses the student directly using 'you'.
SecondP.good_examples:
1) You may have missed applying the product rule to the second term.
1) You do not apply the product rule.
SecondP.bad_examples:
1) You differentiate only x^2 and leave sin(x) unchanged.
2) It is observed that you have included only part of the product in the derivative.

ThirdP.subgroup_info:
The point of view refers to the perspective used to write the feedback message: 
 - 1st person: Written from the instructor's perspective using 'I' statements. 
 - 2nd person: Addresses the student directly using 'you'.
 - 3rd person: Focused on the work itself rather than the instructor or student.
ThirdP.name: Third Person
ThirdP.short: Feedback refers to the work itself rather than the instructor or student.
ThirdP.good_examples:
1) The derivative is missing the second term from the product rule.
2) Only part of the product was differentiated here.
ThirdP.bad_examples:
1) It is observed that the student has failed to fully differentiate the product.
2) It is noted that the solution does not reflect a complete differentiation of the product.

Neutral.subgroup_info:
The interpersonal quality refers to the tone and level of formality used in the feedback message. 
 - Neutral: Emotionally even and matter of fact. 
 - Conversational: Uses natural and approachable language. 
 - Formal: Uses professional and academic language.
Neutral.name: Neutral
Neutral.short: Emotionally even and matter of fact. Signals objectivity and consistency.
Neutral.good_examples:
1) The product rule requires differentiating both terms. The second term is not differentiated here.
2) One term is missing from the product rule application.
Neutral.bad_examples:
1) Great effort! It looks like you may have accidentally missed the second term, but you're on the right track!
2) No worries, it seems like the product rule might not have been fully applied here.

Formal.subgroup_info:
The interpersonal quality refers to the tone and level of formality used in the feedback message. 
 - Neutral: Emotionally even and matter of fact. 
 - Conversational: Uses natural and approachable language. 
 - Formal: Uses professional and academic language.
Formal.name: Formal
Formal.short: Professional and academic. Emphasizes standards and disciplinary norms.
Formal.good_examples:
1) A complete application of the product rule produces two terms. Your solution only includes one.
2) The product rule requires differentiation of both factors. The second term is missing from your solution.
Formal.bad_examples:
1) Hey, looks like you forgot to differentiate the second term when using the product rule!
2) Don't worry, this is a common mistake. Just make sure to apply the product rule to both parts next time!

Conversational.subgroup_info:
The interpersonal quality refers to the tone and level of formality used in the feedback message. 
 - Neutral: Emotionally even and matter of fact. 
 - Conversational: Uses natural and approachable language. 
 - Formal: Uses professional and academic language.
Conversational.name: Conversational
Conversational.good_examples:
1) Both parts of the product need a derivative. It looks like the second term was skipped.
2) Remember that the product rule needs to be applied to both terms, not just the first one.
Conversational.bad_examples:
1) The application of the product rule necessitates the differentiation of each constituent factor in the expression.
2) It is required that the product rule be applied to all constituent terms in the expression.

Direct.subgroup_info:
The directness refers to how explicit or softened the feedback language is. 
 - Direct: Uses explicit and plain language. 
 - Indirect: Uses softened or more conditional language.
Direct.name: Direct
Direct.good_examples:
1) The product rule is not fully applied. Your second term is missing its derivative.
2) Your solution skips the derivative of the second term. Remember, the product rule requires both.
Direct.bad_examples:
1) It is determined that the solution does not reflect a complete application of the product rule to all constituent terms.
2) It looks like the product rule may not have been fully applied here.

Indirect.subgroup_info:
The directness refers to how explicit or softened the feedback language is. 
 - Direct: Uses explicit and plain language. 
 - Indirect: Uses softened or more conditional language.
Indirect.name: Indirect
Indirect.good_examples:
1) It looks like the product rule may not have been fully applied here. Your second term appears to be missing its derivative.
2) It looks like you only differentiated part of the product. The second term might need attention.
Indirect.bad_examples:
1) The product rule is wrong. You failed to differentiate the second term.
2) I observe that the solution may possibly appear to potentially lack a complete application of the product rule to all terms.

TaskFocused.subgroup_info:
Motivational framing refers to how feedback is phrased to support motivation and confidence. 
 - Task-Focused: Emphasizes the work, process, or criteria rather than the student. 
 - Growth-Focused: Frames performance as something that can improve over time. 
 - Wise Feedback: Communicates high standards while affirming the student is capable of meeting them.
TaskFocused.name: Task-Focused
TaskFocused.short: Maintains attention on the work, process, or criteria and avoids personal attribution.
TaskFocused.good_examples:
1) The second factor in the product was not differentiated. Remember, both factors need derivatives.
2) The product rule requires two terms. One term is missing from your derivative.
TaskFocused.bad_examples:
1) You forgot to differentiate the second term, but with practice you'll get the hang of the product rule.
2) It is noted that the solution submitted by the student does not contain a complete derivation of all constituent factors as required by the product rule.

GrowthOriented.subgroup_info:
Motivational framing refers to how feedback is phrased to support motivation and confidence. 
 - Task-Focused: Emphasizes the work, process, or criteria rather than the student. 
 - Growth-Focused: Frames performance as something that can improve over time. 
 - Wise Feedback: Communicates high standards while affirming the student is capable of meeting them.
GrowthOriented.name: Growth-Oriented
GrowthOriented.short: Frames performance as improvable over time.
GrowthOriented.good_examples:
1) The product rule comes up a lot in later work, so it's worth making sure both terms are accounted for.
2) Going back through each step of the product rule to find where it broke down is exactly the kind of practice that'll make this rule stick.
GrowthOriented.bad_examples:
1) It is expected that the student will demonstrate improved application of the product rule in subsequent submissions over the course of the learning period.
2) Some students just struggle with the product rule. It's one of the harder concepts to pick up.

HighExpectation.subgroup_info:
Motivational framing refers to how feedback is phrased to support motivation and confidence. 
 - Task-Focused: Emphasizes the work, process, or criteria rather than the student. 
 - Growth-Focused: Frames performance as something that can improve over time. 
 - Wise Feedback: Communicates high standards while affirming the student is capable of meeting them.
HighExpectation.name: High Expectations + Reassurance
HighExpectation.short: Sets clear standards while affirming that the student is capable of meeting them.
HighExpectation.good_examples:
1) Remember, both terms in a product need to be differentiated. Missing one is a normal slip, but I know that you are fully capable of catching it. Try rewriting the product rule formula and matching each term to your work.
2) This problem requires applying the product rule to both terms. This is a common place to lose a term, and I know that you can absolutely get this right. Go back and write out each part of the product rule separately before combining them.
HighExpectation.bad_examples:
1) You forgot the second term, but don't worry, just try harder next time and you'll probably get it.
2) It is expected that the student will, upon review, demonstrate the capacity to apply the product rule in a manner consistent with the stated academic requirements of this course.
